---
title: "Notebook02"
solutions-release-date: "2026-08-31"
---

### Setup

Run all of the following before starting the notebook.

```{python}
#| tags: [noclear]
#| eval: false
! wget -q -nc https://raw.githubusercontent.com/taylor-arnold/fds2/refs/heads/main/funs.py
```

```{python}
#| tags: [noclear]
import polars as pl
from plotnine import ggplot, aes
from polars import col as c

import funs

ub = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/taylor-arnold/fds2/refs/heads/main/"
```

```{python}
#| tags: [remove]
#| include: false
ub = ""
```

```{python}
#| tags: [noclear]
country = pl.read_csv(ub + "data/countries.csv")
```

### Questions

Last time we used Python as a calculator. Today we load a real dataset and look
at it. The setup cell already read a file called `countries.csv` into a variable
named `country`. This is the same dataset used in the chapter. Each row is one
country and each column is one measurement recorded about that country, such as
its name, its region of the world, its population, and its life expectancy.

One note about the code that loaded the data. The variable `ub` was set in the
setup to the web address where the course files live. Adding it in front of a file
name lets Colab download the file before reading it. You do not need to think about
`ub` beyond that.

Unless a question says otherwise, just run each block and read the result; do not
save it to a variable.

1. Display the dataset by typing its name and running the cell.

```{python}
country
```

2. Look at the output from the previous question. Polars prints the size of the
dataset at the top. How many observations (rows) and how many variables (columns)
does it have?

**Answer**: There are 135 observations and 15 variables. The book calls a row an
observation and a column a variable, so this dataset records 15 measurements for
each of 135 countries.

3. In your own words, what does a single row of this dataset represent, and what
does a single column represent?

**Answer**: A single row represents one country, together with everything we
measured about it. A single column represents one measurement, such as population
or life expectancy, recorded across every country in the dataset.

4. Loading data is done with the `pl.read_csv` function, and it works on any file.
Practice it by loading a second dataset from the book, `data/food.csv`, into a
variable named `food`, and then display it.

```{python}
food = pl.read_csv(ub + "data/food.csv")
food
```

5. Read the size of the `food` dataset from its output. How many rows and columns
does it have?

**Answer**: It has 61 rows and 17 columns, so the book records 17 variables about
each of 61 food items.

6. Look at the short labels printed just under the column names in the `country`
output, such as `str`, `i64`, and `f64`. These are the data types. Columns of
text are marked `str`, and columns of numbers are marked `i64` or `f64`. Name one
text column and one number column.

**Answer**: Text columns include `full_name`, `region`, and `lang`. Number
columns include `pop`, `lexp`, and `gdp`. We will look at what the different
number types mean later in the course; for now it is enough to tell text from
numbers.

7. The chapter lists a few rules for formatting code. One rule is to put a single
space before and after the equals sign and around math operators. The line below
breaks that rule. Rewrite it with correct spacing.

```{python}
#| eval: false
pop=45* 2
```

```{python}
pop = 45 * 2
```

8. Another rule is to name variables with lowercase letters and underscores, and
to start every name with a letter. Of the four names below, which two follow the
book's advice?

```
MyData      my_data      2nd_country      country_pop
```

**Answer**: `my_data` and `country_pop` follow the advice: both are lowercase with
underscores and both start with a letter. `MyData` uses capital letters, which the
book recommends against, and `2nd_country` starts with a number, which Python does
not allow at all.

That is the whole notebook. Starting next time, we will begin reshaping and
sorting datasets rather than just looking at them.
